chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
justification: The 60-year D'Arcy concession created the legal exploration rights that eventually produced the 1908 discovery and the 1909 Anglo-Persian Oil Company (APOC), the entity whose production and profit/royalty flows these two charts track; but the concession itself moved no output or revenue for its first decade.
caveats: Oil production stays at essentially zero for a full decade after the concession (1901-1910), so any link between this specific legal grant and the later production/profit ramp is indirect and mediated by the 1908 discovery and subsequent infrastructure build-out, not a direct same-period effect.

chart_title: AIOC Net Profit, UK Tax Payments & Iran Royalty Payments, 1910-1951
justification: The 60-year D'Arcy concession created the legal exploration rights that eventually produced the 1908 discovery and the 1909 Anglo-Persian Oil Company (APOC), the entity whose production and profit/royalty flows these two charts track; but the concession itself moved no output or revenue for its first decade.
caveats: Oil production stays at essentially zero for a full decade after the concession (1901-1910), so any link between this specific legal grant and the later production/profit ramp is indirect and mediated by the 1908 discovery and subsequent infrastructure build-out, not a direct same-period effect.

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
event_title: First major oil strike at Masjed Soleyman
justification: Iran's oil-production series is flat at zero through 1910, then rises from 0.5 million tonnes in 1911 to over 16 million by 1919 as APOC (founded 1909 on the strength of this discovery) built the pipeline and Abadan refinery needed to bring Masjed Soleyman's output to market -- the discovery is the direct origin of the entire subsequent series.
caveats: The multi-year gap between discovery (1908) and the first non-zero production reading (1911) reflects the time needed for pipeline and refinery construction, so the exact timing of each year's volume also depends on engineering and financing decisions, not the discovery event alone.

chart_title: AIOC Net Profit, UK Tax Payments & Iran Royalty Payments, 1910-1951
event_title: First major oil strike at Masjed Soleyman
justification: The discovery is the founding event for the Anglo-Persian (later Anglo-Iranian) Oil Company, whose net profits, UK tax payments and Iran royalty payments this chart tracks starting in 1910, two years after the strike.
caveats: The chart's start year (1910) is a data-availability cutoff rather than a natural break tied to the discovery; early-decade profit levels also reflect wartime oil demand (WWI) and initial low production volumes, not the discovery alone.

chart_title: Tobacco Monopoly: Regie Concession, Dokhaniyat Conversion & Workforce (1890-1995)
justification: The 1931 law's grant of state trading-company monopolies over sugar, tea, opium, tobacco and cereals is the legal basis for the Iranian tobacco monopoly (Dokhaniyat) whose concession history, conversion and workforce this chart tracks.
caveats: The chart spans 1890-1995 and reflects many later reorganizations of the tobacco monopoly unrelated to this specific 1931 law; exact causal weight of this one law on the chart's workforce/volume figures cannot be isolated from other regulatory changes.

chart_title: AIOC Net Profit, UK Tax Payments & Iran Royalty Payments, 1910-1951
justification: Reza Shah's cancellation of the D'Arcy concession directly disrupted the royalty and profit-sharing framework the chart tracks, forcing renegotiation of Iran's royalty terms with AIOC.
caveats: The dispute was resolved within about six months (May 1933), so any dip or distortion in the annual royalty/profit figures for 1932-33 is hard to disentangle from normal year-to-year fluctuation in oil demand and prices.

chart_title: AIOC Net Profit, UK Tax Payments & Iran Royalty Payments, 1910-1951
lag_description: same year, effects continuing through the 1930s
justification: The new 60-year concession guaranteed Iran a fixed per-ton royalty plus 20% of AIOC's distributed profits, directly resetting the royalty/profit terms this chart measures.
caveats: Iran's royalty income after 1933 also depended heavily on global oil demand and AIOC output decisions, not the contract terms alone; the Depression-era price environment could suppress absolute royalty values even under improved contract terms.

chart_title: Oil Industry Employment by Nationality, 1939-1960
justification: Allied occupation secured Iran's oil fields as a wartime supply corridor, likely keeping foreign (British) staffing and output elevated through the war years even as domestic political control was disrupted.
caveats: Global wartime demand, Allied logistics needs, and pre-existing AIOC production plans are at least as important as the invasion itself in explaining wartime output and staffing levels; hard to isolate the invasion's marginal effect.

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
event_title: Azerbaijan Crisis reaches the UN Security Council
justification: The crisis centered partly on a Soviet demand for a northern Iranian oil concession (refused by the Majlis), and the accompanying political instability and blocked-currency legacy of the wartime occupation plausibly widened official/parallel exchange-rate pressures in this period.
caveats: No direct oil-production or FX-rate mechanism is documented for this specific diplomatic crisis; effects, if any, are swamped by broader postwar reconstruction and inflation dynamics. Treat as background context only.

chart_title: Oil Revenue by Allocation (Budget/NIOC/Plan Org/B.P.C.), 1955/56-1962/63
event_title: Plan Organization established; First Seven-Year Plan
lag_description: pre-dates chart coverage by several years
justification: The Plan Organization created in 1949 is the same institution whose share of oil-revenue allocation this chart tracks starting 1955/56; the chart begins recording the funding channel that this event created.
caveats: The chart's data window (1955/56-1962/63) starts six years after the event and cannot show the First Seven-Year Plan's own 1949-1955 funding pattern; this is background institutional context, not a measured effect.

chart_title: Oil Industry Employment by Nationality, 1939-1960
justification: Nationalization triggered an AIOC-led international boycott and the closure of the Abadan refinery, collapsing Iranian oil output and exports from 1951 until the 1954 Consortium Agreement restored production; it also ends the AIOC royalty/profit series entirely (British operations ceased) and prompted the withdrawal of most foreign technical staff.
caveats: The ocean-trade-tonnage link (excl. petroleum) is weaker and more speculative -- non-oil trade could have moved in either direction as the country adjusted import spending to the sudden loss of oil-financed foreign exchange, and other factors (global commodity prices, domestic harvests) also drove that series.

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
lag_description: 1-2 year lag (production recovery materializes with the 1954 Consortium Agreement)
justification: The coup removed the political impasse over nationalization and cleared the way for the 1954 Consortium Agreement that actually restored output and foreign staffing; the coup itself is a necessary precondition rather than the proximate cause of the production rebound.
caveats: Production did not recover immediately in 1953 -- the actual increase is better attributed to the 1954 Consortium Agreement recorded as a separate event; listing the coup here risks over-crediting a single political event for an outcome finalized a year later by a separate commercial agreement.

chart_title: Oil Industry Employment by Nationality, 1939-1960
lag_description: 1-2 year lag (production recovery materializes with the 1954 Consortium Agreement)
justification: The coup removed the political impasse over nationalization and cleared the way for the 1954 Consortium Agreement that actually restored output and foreign staffing; the coup itself is a necessary precondition rather than the proximate cause of the production rebound.
caveats: Production did not recover immediately in 1953 -- the actual increase is better attributed to the 1954 Consortium Agreement recorded as a separate event; listing the coup here risks over-crediting a single political event for an outcome finalized a year later by a separate commercial agreement.

chart_title: Oil Industry Personnel by Company & Labor Category, 1955-1961
justification: The Consortium Agreement directly restored Iranian oil production and exports after the 1951-54 shutdown, and directly created the consortium wage bill and by-company personnel structure that two of these charts explicitly measure (1954-62 and 1955-61 respectively).
caveats: The ocean-trade-tonnage (excl. petroleum) link is weaker -- broader postwar trade normalization and the Second Seven-Year Plan's investment program likely contributed to any recovery in non-oil trade volumes alongside the oil settlement itself.

chart_title: Oil Industry Employment by Nationality, 1939-1960
justification: The Consortium Agreement directly restored Iranian oil production and exports after the 1951-54 shutdown, and directly created the consortium wage bill and by-company personnel structure that two of these charts explicitly measure (1954-62 and 1955-61 respectively).
caveats: The ocean-trade-tonnage (excl. petroleum) link is weaker -- broader postwar trade normalization and the Second Seven-Year Plan's investment program likely contributed to any recovery in non-oil trade volumes alongside the oil settlement itself.

chart_title: Oil Revenue by Allocation (Budget/NIOC/Plan Org/B.P.C.), 1955/56-1962/63
lag_description: same year, continuing through the plan's 1955-1962 span
justification: The plan was financed substantially by oil revenue following the 1954 Consortium Agreement, with allocations to the Plan Organization exactly matching the budget categories (Budget/NIOC/Plan Org/B.P.C.) this chart records for the same 1955/56-1962/63 window.
caveats: The allocation shares reflect ongoing budget-negotiation dynamics each year, not solely the plan's initial design; the 1957-59 distribution chart is a narrower three-year slice and may not represent the plan's allocation pattern across its full run.

chart_title: Oil Revenue Distribution by Recipient (% share), 1957-1959
lag_description: same year, continuing through the plan's 1955-1962 span
justification: The plan was financed substantially by oil revenue following the 1954 Consortium Agreement, with allocations to the Plan Organization exactly matching the budget categories (Budget/NIOC/Plan Org/B.P.C.) this chart records for the same 1955/56-1962/63 window.
caveats: The allocation shares reflect ongoing budget-negotiation dynamics each year, not solely the plan's initial design; the 1957-59 distribution chart is a narrower three-year slice and may not represent the plan's allocation pattern across its full run.

chart_title: Central Bank Assets to GDP (World Bank GFDD)
lag_description: immediate institutional start, gradual growth over decades
justification: Bank Markazi's creation in May 1960 is the founding event for the central-bank balance sheet and the modern monetary aggregates (broad money/liquid liabilities) that these GFDD series begin tracking that same year.
caveats: The chart values reflect decades of subsequent monetary policy, financial deepening, and (post-1978) very different institutional conditions; the 1960 founding explains why the series starts then, not the specific trajectory of the ratios afterward.

caveats: The chart values reflect decades of subsequent monetary policy, financial deepening, and (post-1978) very different institutional conditions; the 1960 founding explains why the series starts then, not the specific trajectory of the ratios afterward.

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
event_title: OPEC founded with Iran as charter member
justification: OPEC's founding gave Iran a coordinating body for petroleum output and pricing policy that would later matter greatly for its production decisions (notably in the 1970s), but had no immediate production effect in 1960.
caveats: OPEC exerted negligible influence on member output in its first decade; any visible movement in Iranian production around 1960 is far better explained by post-Consortium-Agreement capacity expansion than by OPEC membership itself.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: First IMF stand-by arrangement and stabilization program
justification: The IMF-backed austerity program explicitly curbed bank credit expansion and government spending through 1962 to defend reserves and the currency, which is exactly what the private-credit-to-GDP and broad-money (liquid liabilities) series measure, and directly targeted the exchange-rate pressure the FX chart tracks.
caveats: The GFDD monetary series only begin in 1960/61, giving very little pre-program baseline to confirm the credit slowdown against; broader global commodity-price and oil-revenue trends also affected Iran's reserves and credit conditions independent of the IMF program.

chart_title: Private Credit by Deposit Money Banks to GDP (World Bank GFDD)
event_title: First IMF stand-by arrangement and stabilization program
justification: The IMF-backed austerity program explicitly curbed bank credit expansion and government spending through 1962 to defend reserves and the currency, which is exactly what the private-credit-to-GDP and broad-money (liquid liabilities) series measure, and directly targeted the exchange-rate pressure the FX chart tracks.
caveats: The GFDD monetary series only begin in 1960/61, giving very little pre-program baseline to confirm the credit slowdown against; broader global commodity-price and oil-revenue trends also affected Iran's reserves and credit conditions independent of the IMF program.

chart_title: Liquid Liabilities (Broad Money) (World Bank GFDD)
event_title: First IMF stand-by arrangement and stabilization program
justification: The IMF-backed austerity program explicitly curbed bank credit expansion and government spending through 1962 to defend reserves and the currency, which is exactly what the private-credit-to-GDP and broad-money (liquid liabilities) series measure, and directly targeted the exchange-rate pressure the FX chart tracks.
caveats: The GFDD monetary series only begin in 1960/61, giving very little pre-program baseline to confirm the credit slowdown against; broader global commodity-price and oil-revenue trends also affected Iran's reserves and credit conditions independent of the IMF program.

chart_title: Government Debt to the Central Bank
justification: Collapsing oil revenue during an active war reduced fiscal space, plausibly pushing the wartime government toward greater borrowing from the central bank to cover the gap.
caveats: War spending itself (not the price collapse specifically) is the dominant driver of 1980s central-bank financing; hard to isolate the price-collapse contribution from war-driven deficits.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: A sudden drop in dollar oil earnings reduces the central bank's ability to supply hard currency at the official rate, typically widening the gap to the parallel/black-market rate.
caveats: The official-parallel FX gap in this period is driven mainly by wartime foreign-exchange rationing and multiple exchange rates, not the oil price alone.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
justification: January 1992 'shock therapy' price liberalization in the newly independent Russia removed Soviet-era price controls essentially overnight, producing a documented inflation spike (roughly 2,500% in 1992).
caveats: Concurrent ruble-zone breakdown, supply-chain collapse among former Soviet republics, and loose monetary financing of the deficit all contributed alongside price liberalization itself.

chart_title: GDP growth
lag_description: same year, multi-year decline through the 1990s
justification: The chaotic dismantling of central planning and loss of intra-Soviet trade links drove a sustained Russian output collapse (the 'transformational recession') through the 1990s.
caveats: The decline continued for most of the decade, so later years reflect additional shocks (1998 default) rather than the dissolution alone.

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: Emerging-market contagion from the peso collapse triggered a bank run and capital flight in Argentina, contributing to Argentina's 1995 recession under its dollar-pegged convertibility regime.
caveats: Argentina's convertibility-plan vulnerabilities (rigid peg, weak bank capitalization) were pre-existing; the Tequila shock was a trigger among several structural weaknesses, not the sole cause.

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: Contagion from the Thai baht collapse forced South Korea into an IMF program in late 1997; Korean GDP contracted roughly 5.5% in 1998 as credit and investment collapsed.
caveats: Domestic corporate over-leverage (chaebol debt) and weak bank supervision were pre-existing vulnerabilities the crisis exposed rather than created.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
justification: The won's sharp depreciation during the crisis raised import prices, pushing Korean consumer-price inflation up to roughly 7-8% in 1998 before it subsided.
caveats: Inflation eased quickly once the won stabilized in 1999, consistent with a one-off pass-through effect rather than a persistent inflation regime change.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
event_title: Russian default and LTCM crisis trigger global contagion
justification: The August 1998 ruble devaluation and domestic debt default drove Russian consumer prices up roughly 84% in 1999 as the currency collapse passed through to import and domestic prices.
caveats: Chronic pre-1998 fiscal weakness and reliance on short-term GKO debt financing set up the default; low oil prices that year compounded the fiscal strain.

chart_title: GDP growth
event_title: Russian default and LTCM crisis trigger global contagion
justification: The default and ruble devaluation triggered a banking-system collapse and a roughly 5.3% contraction in Russian GDP in 1998.
caveats: Concurrent low world oil prices (a Russian export mainstay) independently depressed output; the two shocks are hard to fully separate in the same-year GDP figure.

chart_title: Real interest rate
justification: Locking Italy's exchange rate and monetary policy into the euro convergence process eliminated the currency-risk and inflation-risk premium that had kept Italian interest rates elevated, driving real rates down toward German/core-euro levels.
caveats: Broader European disinflation and independent Bank of Italy tightening in the run-up to convergence also pushed rates down; the euro-launch effect is one strand of a multi-year convergence trend.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
lag_description: gradual over the period, same year to 1 year lag
justification: Crude prices rising from ~$30 to $147/barrel between 2003 and mid-2008 directly lifted Iran's oil rents (% of GDP) and dollar goods-export earnings, which are overwhelmingly oil-export-driven.
caveats: Iranian export volumes also fluctuated with OPEC quota decisions and domestic field investment, so the revenue windfall is not price alone.

chart_title: Goods exports
lag_description: gradual over the period, same year to 1 year lag
justification: Crude prices rising from ~$30 to $147/barrel between 2003 and mid-2008 directly lifted Iran's oil rents (% of GDP) and dollar goods-export earnings, which are overwhelmingly oil-export-driven.
caveats: Iranian export volumes also fluctuated with OPEC quota decisions and domestic field investment, so the revenue windfall is not price alone.

chart_title: Money Supply and Monetary Base
justification: The oil windfall financed large government spending and central-bank credit expansion under the Ahmadinejad administration, a widely cited driver of the mid-2000s surge in Iranian money supply and monetary base.
caveats: Domestic monetary and credit policy choices (subsidized lending, the Maskan-e Mehr housing scheme) were discretionary decisions layered on top of the oil windfall, not an automatic mechanical link.

chart_title: Net barter terms of trade index
lag_description: same year, 2005-2008 portion of the cycle
justification: Rising oil prices relative to import prices mechanically improved Iran's net barter terms of trade over 2005-2008, the portion of the cycle this series covers.
caveats: Series only begins in 2005, so it captures the back half of the super-cycle, not the full 2003 run-up.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
justification: The US-led invasion halted roughly 2 million barrels/day of Iraqi oil production and exports in 2003, directly collapsing Iraq's oil rents and goods-export revenue for the year.
caveats: Post-invasion looting, infrastructure sabotage, and sanctions-era underinvestment also suppressed Iraqi output independent of the invasion's immediate combat effects.

chart_title: Goods exports
justification: The US-led invasion halted roughly 2 million barrels/day of Iraqi oil production and exports in 2003, directly collapsing Iraq's oil rents and goods-export revenue for the year.
caveats: Post-invasion looting, infrastructure sabotage, and sanctions-era underinvestment also suppressed Iraqi output independent of the invasion's immediate combat effects.

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: Iraqi GDP swung sharply around the invasion — an initial wartime contraction followed by a chaotic, aid- and oil-financed rebound — reflecting the direct disruption of the war and occupation.
caveats: Post-2003 Iraqi GDP is exceptionally volatile and driven by security conditions, reconstruction spending, and oil-price swings together, making a single-event attribution imprecise.

chart_title: Goods exports
event_title: Global Financial Crisis — Lehman Brothers collapse
justification: The synchronized global recession crashed oil prices from ~$147 to ~$40/barrel within months of the Lehman collapse, directly cutting Iran's oil rents and goods-export earnings.
caveats: OPEC production-cut responses partially offset the price collapse's revenue impact by early 2009; the recovery trajectory reflects both price and policy response.

chart_title: GDP growth
event_title: Global Financial Crisis — Lehman Brothers collapse
justification: The credit freeze and demand collapse drove recessions across Spain, Italy, the US, and Russia in 2008-2009 (Spain and Italy entering prolonged downturns), all captured in this shared GDP-growth series.
caveats: Spain and Italy's downturns were prolonged well past the initial 2008-09 shock by their own banking and sovereign-debt vulnerabilities (see European sovereign debt crisis), so post-2009 weakness has additional causes.

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: The fiscal-sustainability crisis and ensuing austerity programs drove Spain and Italy into a multi-year recession/stagnation from 2010-2013, both captured in this shared GDP-growth series.
caveats: Spain's crisis was compounded by its own housing-bubble bust and bank recapitalization needs, distinct from the fiscal-crisis mechanism proper; Italy's stagnation predates 2010 and has structural roots.

chart_title: Government Net Debt (assets netted)
justification: Recession-driven revenue shortfalls plus crisis-response spending and bank bailouts pushed Spanish and Italian net government debt sharply higher through the crisis years.
caveats: Italy's debt-to-GDP ratio was already high before 2010 for structural reasons; the crisis accelerated but did not originate Italy's debt trajectory.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
justification: Over 2 million barrels/day of regional production lost (Libya, Syria, Yemen) pushed Brent from $92 to $120/barrel by April 2011, lifting oil rents for exporters in the roster including Saudi Arabia and, to a lesser extent, Iran.
caveats: For Iran specifically the windfall was partly offset by tightening US/EU sanctions that same year targeting its central bank and oil exports, muddying the net effect on Iranian series; the price effect is clearer for Saudi Arabia.

chart_title: Goods exports
justification: Oil prices falling from ~$115 to below $30/barrel amid US shale supply growth and OPEC's decision not to cut output directly cut Iran's oil rents and goods-export revenue over 2014-2016.
caveats: Iran's exports over the same window were also constrained by nuclear-related sanctions until JCPOA implementation in January 2016, so pre-2016 export levels reflect sanctions as much as price.

chart_title: Government Primary Balance (excl. interest)
justification: The collapse in oil revenue widened Iran's fiscal deficit, a widely cited driver of primary-balance deterioration in this period.
caveats: Subsidy-reform costs and pre-existing structural spending commitments also weighed on the primary balance independent of the oil-price shock.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Reduced dollar oil earnings tightened the central bank's hard-currency supply, a plausible contributor to renewed pressure on the parallel-market premium in 2014-2015.
caveats: This period also saw active nuclear negotiations shaping FX-market expectations independent of the oil price itself, making the two hard to disentangle.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
lag_description: 6 months to 1 year lag (implementation from Jan 2016)
justification: Expectation of sanctions relief and the January 2016 Implementation Day narrowed the gap between Iran's official and parallel exchange rates as capital-flight pressure eased.
caveats: The improvement was temporary and partly reflects government FX-market intervention around the deal's signing rather than the deal's economic terms alone.

chart_title: Goods exports
justification: Lifting of nuclear-related EU/UN oil and shipping-insurance sanctions let Iranian crude exports roughly double from under 1 million to over 2 million barrels/day by 2017, directly lifting export revenue and oil rents.
caveats: Global oil prices were still relatively low in 2016 (post-2014 collapse), so the volume rebound was partly offset by weak per-barrel prices.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
justification: Lifting of nuclear-related EU/UN oil and shipping-insurance sanctions let Iranian crude exports roughly double from under 1 million to over 2 million barrels/day by 2017, directly lifting export revenue and oil rents.
caveats: Global oil prices were still relatively low in 2016 (post-2014 collapse), so the volume rebound was partly offset by weak per-barrel prices.

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: The oil-export rebound following sanctions relief drove a sharp Iranian GDP growth rebound (roughly 12-13%) in 2016, widely attributed to JCPOA implementation.
caveats: The 2016 growth spike was driven overwhelmingly by the oil sector; non-oil growth remained comparatively modest, so the headline figure overstates broad-based recovery.

chart_title: Total reserves in months of imports
justification: Release of roughly $100bn in frozen Iranian assets after IAEA-verified implementation began in January 2016 directly boosted reserve availability.
caveats: Much of the 'frozen assets' figure represented restricted, already-earned oil receivables rather than freely disposable new reserves, so the practical liquidity gain was smaller than the headline number.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Reimposed US sanctions and the threat of secondary sanctions on any bank dealing with Iran triggered a well-documented rial collapse in the parallel market in 2018, with the gap to the official rate widening several-fold within months.
caveats: Domestic mismanagement of a dual/multiple exchange-rate system introduced by the government in April 2018 (just before the US withdrawal) also directly fed the parallel-market blowout.

chart_title: Goods exports
justification: Reimposition of oil sanctions (full 'snapback' by November 2018) cut Iranian crude exports from roughly 2.5 million to under 0.5 million barrels/day by 2019-2020, directly collapsing export revenue and oil rents.
caveats: A portion of the 2019-2020 decline coincides with the COVID-19 oil-demand collapse, which independently depressed both price and volume from March 2020 onward.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
justification: Reimposition of oil sanctions (full 'snapback' by November 2018) cut Iranian crude exports from roughly 2.5 million to under 0.5 million barrels/day by 2019-2020, directly collapsing export revenue and oil rents.
caveats: A portion of the 2019-2020 decline coincides with the COVID-19 oil-demand collapse, which independently depressed both price and volume from March 2020 onward.

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: Re-isolation from the dollar banking system and the oil-export collapse drove Iran into recession, with GDP contracting roughly 6% in 2018 and 7% in 2019, widely attributed to the sanctions snapback.
caveats: Chronic banking-sector fragility and pre-existing structural weaknesses in the Iranian economy also contributed to the downturn's depth.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
justification: The rial collapse passed through rapidly to import and domestic prices, pushing Iranian consumer-price inflation from roughly 10% to over 40% by 2019, a well-documented sanctions-driven spike.
caveats: Loose monetary financing of the budget deficit amid falling oil revenue was a compounding driver of inflation alongside the exchange-rate shock.

chart_title: Bahar Azadi Gold Coin Price, Rial, Daily (2013-07-22 to 2026-07-11)
justification: Gold coins are a standard rial-denominated hedge in Iran; their rial price tracks the currency's collapse closely, and both series show a sharp rise coinciding with the sanctions snapback and rial devaluation from mid-2018.
caveats: Global gold-dollar prices also moved over this period, so part of the rial-price rise reflects world gold-market conditions rather than the rial alone; the two effects are not separated in this series.

chart_title: Emami Gold Coin Price, Rial, Daily (2010-04-04 to 2026-07-11)
justification: Gold coins are a standard rial-denominated hedge in Iran; their rial price tracks the currency's collapse closely, and both series show a sharp rise coinciding with the sanctions snapback and rial devaluation from mid-2018.
caveats: Global gold-dollar prices also moved over this period, so part of the rial-price rise reflects world gold-market conditions rather than the rial alone; the two effects are not separated in this series.

chart_title: Cause of death, by other COVID-19 pandemic-related outcomes (% of total)
justification: This series directly measures the share of deaths attributed to COVID-19-pandemic-related outcomes, the pandemic's most direct measurable effect.
caveats: Reported COVID death shares are sensitive to each country's testing and cause-of-death coding practices, which varied widely (Iran and Russia both faced credible undercount allegations).

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: Synchronized global lockdowns collapsed travel and industrial oil demand and triggered a worldwide recession in 2020, directly depressing both global crude prices (including Iran's OPEC reference-basket price) and GDP growth across the roster.
caveats: For Iran specifically, US sanctions were already suppressing oil exports before the pandemic, so the 2020 price/output effects layer on top of a pre-existing sanctions shock rather than starting from a normal baseline.

chart_title: Iran Heavy Crude Oil Spot Price, OPEC Reference Basket (2020-2024)
justification: Synchronized global lockdowns collapsed travel and industrial oil demand and triggered a worldwide recession in 2020, directly depressing both global crude prices (including Iran's OPEC reference-basket price) and GDP growth across the roster.
caveats: For Iran specifically, US sanctions were already suppressing oil exports before the pandemic, so the 2020 price/output effects layer on top of a pre-existing sanctions shock rather than starting from a normal baseline.

chart_title: Iran Heavy Crude Oil Spot Price, OPEC Reference Basket (2020-2024)
justification: The WTI negative-settlement event was the sharpest single data point of the broader April 2020 COVID oil-demand collapse and storage-capacity crunch, a period in which Iran's OPEC reference-basket price also fell to multi-year lows.
caveats: Iranian heavy crude is priced and delivered very differently from landlocked US WTI (no negative settlement occurred for seaborne grades); the link here is a shared demand-collapse driver, not the same market mechanism.

chart_title: Iran Heavy Crude Oil Spot Price, OPEC Reference Basket (2020-2024)
justification: Sweeping Western sanctions on Russian energy exports tightened global oil supply and lifted prices in 2022, a tailwind that also raised the price Iran received for the crude it could still sell despite its own sanctions.
caveats: Iran's exports remained sanctioned throughout, so it captured the price benefit only on volumes it could move (mainly to China at a discount), not the full global price gain.

chart_title: GDP growth
justification: Sweeping Western financial and export-control sanctions imposed after the invasion drove a Russian GDP contraction (roughly -1 to -2%) in 2022.
caveats: The contraction was smaller and shorter than initially forecast, as Russia rerouted trade toward Asia and ran expansionary wartime fiscal policy, partly offsetting the sanctions shock.

chart_title: Current account balance
justification: High energy prices combined with a sanctions-driven collapse in Russian imports pushed Russia's current account surplus to record levels in 2022.
caveats: The imports collapse (a supply-side sanctions effect) mechanically inflates the current account balance even as overall trade volumes shrank, so a rising balance here does not indicate a healthier economy.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
justification: Energy and food-price shocks from the war drove a documented inflation surge in Spain and Italy in 2022, both captured in this shared inflation series.
caveats: Post-pandemic supply-chain pressures and pre-existing monetary stimulus were already pushing European inflation up before the invasion; the war compounded rather than started the surge.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: Fastest Federal Reserve tightening cycle since the 1980s
justification: Broad-based dollar strength during the Fed's tightening cycle put downward pressure on many emerging-market currencies globally, a secondary headwind on the rial's parallel-market value alongside Iran's own sanctions and inflation dynamics.
caveats: Iran's currency depreciation over this period is overwhelmingly explained by domestic sanctions, inflation, and money-supply growth; the Fed-tightening channel is a minor, hard-to-isolate contributor given Iran's limited integration with global capital markets.

chart_title: Use of IMF credit
justification: Argentina's one-off ~$9.5bn payment from central-bank reserves in Jan 2006 extinguished essentially all outstanding IMF credit, driving Argentina's 'Use of IMF credit' line to zero from 2006 onward.
caveats: This is a multi-country comparator chart (ARG, IRN, IRQ, RUS, TUR); the effect is specific to Argentina's own series and says nothing about the other countries plotted alongside it.

chart_title: IMF repurchases and charges
justification: The lump-sum prepayment itself registers as a one-time spike in 'IMF repurchases and charges' (debt service to the Fund) for Argentina in 2006, before falling to zero afterward since no IMF debt remained.
caveats: A single-year spike followed by zero can be hard to read correctly without isolating Argentina's line from the other comparator countries in the same chart.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
justification: From 2007 Argentina's officially reported CPI inflation was manipulated downward by INDEC under political pressure, so Argentina's line in this comparator inflation chart understates true price growth for roughly 2007-2015.
caveats: This is a data-integrity issue, not a real macroeconomic effect: independent and later-revised INDEC estimates suggest true inflation ran materially above the official figures shown during this window.

chart_title: Debt service on external debt, public and publicly guaranteed (PPG)
justification: Argentina's inability to pay holdout bondholders after Judge Griesa's ruling forced a technical default on restructured debt, disrupting scheduled payments captured in the public/publicly-guaranteed debt-service series.
caveats: WDI debt-service data can still record contractually accrued amounts regardless of actual payment status, so the measured effect may understate the real payment disruption; this is Argentina's line within a multi-country comparator chart.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
event_title: INDEC statistical emergency declared; blackout begins to end
justification: The six-month suspension of CPI publication and subsequent stopgap index in 2016 creates a visible data-quality break in Argentina's reported inflation series just as the Macri government tries to restore credible statistics.
caveats: This is a data-availability/quality event rather than a real economic shock in itself; the genuine inflation spike visible in this same period is driven by Macri's actual devaluation and subsidy cuts, not by the statistical reform.

chart_title: Use of IMF credit
event_title: IMF approves record $50 billion Stand-By Arrangement
justification: Successive disbursements under the 2018 Stand-By Arrangement (later expanded to $57bn) directly increased Argentina's outstanding IMF credit, visible as a sharp rise in the 'Use of IMF credit' series from 2018.
caveats: Causality runs both ways here: the SBA is itself a response to the 2018 peso crisis, and also a direct cause of the debt-stock rise shown in the chart.

chart_title: Debt service on external debt, total
justification: Restructuring roughly $65bn in foreign-law bonds extended maturities and cut near-term coupons, mechanically lowering Argentina's scheduled total external debt service in the years right after the September 2020 settlement.
caveats: The relatively small haircut (~5%) means total debt stock changed little; the clearer visible effect is on near-term debt-service burden/timing rather than on the debt-stock level.

chart_title: Use of IMF credit
event_title: IMF approves $44 billion Extended Fund Facility
justification: The EFF restructured repayment of the 2018 SBA rather than adding large fresh net financing, reshaping (longer maturities) rather than simply increasing Argentina's outstanding IMF credit stock shown in this series.
caveats: Isolating a restructuring's effect on the debt-stock series from what the original 2018 SBA repayment schedule would have produced anyway requires a counterfactual the WDI data alone cannot supply.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
event_title: Milei devalues peso 54% in shock-therapy program
justification: The 54% official devaluation raised import and tradeable-goods costs, feeding directly into Argentina's already-high consumer-price inflation visible in this comparator chart for 2023-2024.
caveats: Argentina's inflation was already running above 140% before the devaluation due to unrelated fiscal deficit monetization; the devaluation is one contributing shock layered on a pre-existing high-inflation trajectory.

chart_title: AIOC Net Profit, UK Tax Payments & Iran Royalty Payments, 1910-1951
event_title: Great Depression becomes a global crisis
justification: Collapsing global oil demand and prices during the Depression cut Anglo-Persian Oil Company profits in the early 1930s, sharply reducing Iran's profit-based royalty payments and helping precipitate Reza Shah's 1932 cancellation of the D'Arcy concession over inadequate royalties.
caveats: The chart's earliest data point is a lumped 1910-32 aggregate rather than an annual series, so the Depression-era decline cannot be cleanly isolated year-by-year from the concession dispute, which also had independent political drivers.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: Bretton Woods Conference founds the IMF and World Bank
justification: Bretton Woods established the fixed-but-adjustable par-value exchange-rate system that Iran's officially quoted rial/dollar rate operated within for the following quarter-century, visible as the flat, administratively-set 'Official' rate through the 1950s-60s in this chart.
caveats: This is a background institutional condition, not a discrete price-moving event for Iran; Iran's own currency-management choices and oil-revenue inflows mattered far more for the actual rate path than IMF/World Bank membership itself.

chart_title: Oil Revenue by Allocation (Budget/NIOC/Plan Org/B.P.C.), 1955/56-1962/63
justification: The Suez Canal closure (Nov 1956-Apr 1957) temporarily diverted demand toward non-Suez-dependent crude and briefly firmed prices, adding a modest tailwind to Iran's Consortium-era oil revenues recorded in this budget-allocation series around 1956/57.
caveats: This period is dominated by the much larger effect of the 1954 Consortium Agreement ramping up Iranian production from a low post-nationalization base, which likely swamps any Suez-specific bump in the annual figures.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: Nixon Shock — end of dollar-gold convertibility
justification: The collapse of Bretton Woods forced a renegotiation of gold/dollar par values worldwide; Iran's officially pegged rial rate was adjusted in the Smithsonian (Dec 1971) and February 1973 realignments that followed, visible as step-changes in the 'Official' rate series around 1972-73.
caveats: Iran's rate was administratively set by the central bank rather than market-determined, so any change reflects a discrete policy decision responding to the new international system rather than continuous market repricing; other bilateral considerations also affected timing.

chart_title: Wheat
justification: The near-tripling of world grain prices by 1974 fed through to Iran's domestic wheat prices and to overall consumer inflation, compounding price pressure already building from the concurrent 1973-74 oil-revenue boom and demand overheating.
caveats: 1973-74 Iranian inflation and wheat prices were also driven heavily by the domestic oil-revenue-fueled spending boom; the global food crisis is one contributing external channel among several, not cleanly separable in the aggregate series.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
justification: The near-tripling of world grain prices by 1974 fed through to Iran's domestic wheat prices and to overall consumer inflation, compounding price pressure already building from the concurrent 1973-74 oil-revenue boom and demand overheating.
caveats: 1973-74 Iranian inflation and wheat prices were also driven heavily by the domestic oil-revenue-fueled spending boom; the global food crisis is one contributing external channel among several, not cleanly separable in the aggregate series.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
event_title: Yom Kippur War and 1973 oil embargo
justification: OPEC's embargo-driven roughly fourfold oil-price increase from late 1973 is the direct cause of oil rents jumping from 18.6% of Iran's GDP in 1973 to 47.4% in 1974 -- the single largest jump in the entire series -- while Iran (not itself an embargo participant) also raised output to capture the higher prices.
caveats: Iran continued selling to the US and other embargoed destinations and was not an embargo participant itself; the effect here runs through the global price shock OPEC engineered, and ongoing field-development investment also contributed to the production rise.

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
event_title: Yom Kippur War and 1973 oil embargo
justification: OPEC's embargo-driven roughly fourfold oil-price increase from late 1973 is the direct cause of oil rents jumping from 18.6% of Iran's GDP in 1973 to 47.4% in 1974 -- the single largest jump in the entire series -- while Iran (not itself an embargo participant) also raised output to capture the higher prices.
caveats: Iran continued selling to the US and other embargoed destinations and was not an embargo participant itself; the effect here runs through the global price shock OPEC engineered, and ongoing field-development investment also contributed to the production rise.

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
justification: The event's own mechanism is Iran's own output collapse: revolutionary strikes and turmoil cut Iran's production from about 3.08m barrels/day-equivalent in 1978 to 1.87m in 1979 and 0.86m in 1980 -- the supply disruption that itself constituted the global 'second oil shock.'
caveats: This is close to the most direct link possible since Iran's own production is the described source of the shock, not a downstream effect; the Iran-Iraq War from September 1980 further compounds the decline into 1980-81, so not all of the drop is attributable to the Revolution alone.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
justification: Even as Iran's own production volume fell, the roughly doubling of world oil prices in 1979-80 kept oil rents as a share of GDP elevated (36.3% in 1979, 34.1% in 1980) rather than collapsing in line with volume, since the price effect partly offset the volume loss.
caveats: Iran's GDP was also contracting sharply post-revolution, which mechanically supports the oil-rents ratio; disentangling the price-shock effect from the GDP-denominator effect is not possible from this series alone.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: A black-market premium over the fixed official rial rate appears clearly in the data starting 1979 (an 81% premium that year, rising further through 1981), coinciding with the political and oil-supply turmoil this event describes.
caveats: The FX gap is driven far more directly by the Revolution's political upheaval, capital flight, and the subsequent 1979-81 US hostage-crisis asset freeze than by the global oil-price shock specifically; treat this as a coincident political-economy effect rather than a direct oil-price transmission channel.

chart_title: External debt stocks, total
justification: Volcker-era global interest-rate increases raised debt-service costs on variable-rate external debt across emerging markets in the early 1980s, a channel that in principle applies to Iran's external debt stock shown in this comparator series.
caveats: Iran's 1979-85 external-debt path is dominated by far larger idiosyncratic shocks -- the US asset freeze after the hostage crisis, war financing from 1980, and oil-revenue swings -- that make any Volcker-specific interest-rate effect essentially undetectable in the aggregate series; confidence is low for this reason.

chart_title: External debt stocks, total
justification: Mexico's August 1982 default triggered a region-wide freeze in voluntary lending and forced Argentina (already fragile after its own 1980-82 banking crisis) into IMF-brokered rescheduling, a dynamic reflected in Argentina's external-debt-stock line within this multi-country comparator chart through the 1980s 'lost decade.'
caveats: Argentina's debt buildup had domestic roots (military-government borrowing, the 1980-82 banking collapse) predating the Mexican trigger; the crisis accelerated and regionalized an existing vulnerability rather than creating it outright. This is Argentina's line specifically, not Iran's.

chart_title: Debt service on external debt, total
justification: Debt-service payments by Argentina fell relative to originally scheduled obligations as the country entered serial reschedulings following the region-wide 1982 crisis, visible in Argentina's line within this comparator debt-service series.
caveats: WDI debt-service data can reflect rescheduled/restructured terms rather than original contractual obligations, complicating a clean before/after read; other Latin American dynamics not captured in this Iran-focused database also shaped the outcome.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
justification: Dollar depreciation engineered by the Plaza Accord is cited as a contributing factor in the 1986 oil-price collapse; Iran's oil rents as a share of GDP fell sharply from 13.0% in 1985 to 5.3% in 1986, consistent with the broader price crash.
caveats: The dominant driver of the 1986 oil-price collapse was Saudi Arabia abandoning its swing-producer role and flooding the market, not the Plaza Accord's dollar depreciation; Plaza is at most a secondary contributing channel.

chart_title: Oil Production (physical volume)
event_title: World War I begins — global gold standard and trade disruption
justification: Britain's pre-war conversion of the Royal Navy to oil power made Persian oil strategically important just as WWI began; Iranian production rose steadily through the war years (about 4.4 thousand tonnes in 1914 to 13 in 1918) as British wartime demand grew.
caveats: This is a smooth continuation of D'Arcy-concession field development that began years before the war, with no visible war-specific inflection in the annual data; the gold-standard suspension and European trade disruption this event centers on had little direct bearing on Iran, which sat outside the belligerent core economies.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: UNSC Resolution 1803 tightens bank and cargo vigilance
justification: Resolution 1803 mandated cargo inspection and bank vigilance over Iranian transactions, an early step in the sanctions ratchet that eventually pressured the rial and foreign investment, but its own direct effect was modest.
caveats: 1803 alone imposed no blocking sanctions; any FX or investment movement in 2008 is more plausibly driven by the global financial crisis and oil-price swings than by this resolution specifically.

chart_title: Foreign direct investment, net
event_title: UNSC Resolution 1803 tightens bank and cargo vigilance
justification: Resolution 1803 mandated cargo inspection and bank vigilance over Iranian transactions, an early step in the sanctions ratchet that eventually pressured the rial and foreign investment, but its own direct effect was modest.
caveats: 1803 alone imposed no blocking sanctions; any FX or investment movement in 2008 is more plausibly driven by the global financial crisis and oil-price swings than by this resolution specifically.

chart_title: Market capitalization of listed domestic companies
event_title: Telecommunication Company of Iran privatized to IRGC-linked consortium
justification: The $7.8bn TCI stake sale was one of the largest single transactions in Tehran Stock Exchange history at the time, mechanically adding to listed market capitalization and trading turnover in 2009.
caveats: WDI market-cap and turnover series are annual aggregates that also move with the broader TSE cycle (inflation-driven equity rallies, other listings), so the TCI deal is only one of several contributors to any single-year jump.

chart_title: Stocks traded, total value
event_title: Telecommunication Company of Iran privatized to IRGC-linked consortium
justification: The $7.8bn TCI stake sale was one of the largest single transactions in Tehran Stock Exchange history at the time, mechanically adding to listed market capitalization and trading turnover in 2009.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Resolution 1929 expanded financial-sector and IRGC-linked sanctions, adding to the cumulative pressure that widened the official/parallel FX gap and deterred foreign investment over the following years.
caveats: 1929's effect is impossible to separate from the concurrent CISADA sanctions and general oil-price environment of 2010-11; treat as one contributor among several rather than a standalone shock.

chart_title: Foreign direct investment, net

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: CISADA expands US sanctions on fuel and financial sectors
justification: CISADA targeted firms supplying Iran with refined petroleum and discouraged banking ties, adding to pressure on fuel-related export earnings and the exchange rate that intensified through 2011-12.
caveats: Effects overlap heavily with UNSCR 1929 and later NDAA/EU measures passed within the same 18 months; isolating CISADA's specific contribution from the broader sanctions escalation is not credible with annual data.

chart_title: Fuel exports (% of merchandise exports)
event_title: CISADA expands US sanctions on fuel and financial sectors
justification: CISADA targeted firms supplying Iran with refined petroleum and discouraged banking ties, adding to pressure on fuel-related export earnings and the exchange rate that intensified through 2011-12.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
lag_description: same year, with demand-response effects over 1-3 years
justification: Energy prices were raised 3-9x and bread prices doubled overnight while ~90% of households were simultaneously enrolled in a cash-transfer program costing ~10% of GDP -- a single administrative act that directly repriced the CPI/WPI basket, restructured government subsidy/transfer spending, and mechanically expanded social-protection program coverage to near-universal levels.
caveats: CPI and WPI also move with the FX rate and global commodity prices in the same period; energy-consumption declines reflect a gradual demand response and are also affected by weather, GDP growth, and later phase-two price hikes in 2014, so the 2010 reform is a major but not sole driver of the multi-year consumption trend.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: NDAA Section 1245 targets Central Bank of Iran
justification: Threatening foreign banks conducting significant CBI transactions with loss of US correspondent access directly targeted Iran's ability to repatriate oil revenue, triggering rial-depreciation expectations that show up as a widening official/parallel gap and a jump in gold-coin (dollar-hedge) demand into 2012.
caveats: NDAA 1245 was announced alongside the looming EU oil embargo and SWIFT disconnection (both early 2012), so the FX and gold-price reaction in this window reflects anticipation of the full sanctions package, not this provision alone.

chart_title: Fuel exports (% of merchandise exports)
lag_description: same year, full effect by mid-2012 wind-down deadline
justification: An EU-wide ban on Iranian crude and petroleum-product imports plus a freeze on CBI assets directly cut a major buyer of Iranian oil, reducing fuel-export receipts, oil rents as a share of GDP, and the current-account balance, while fueling the rial's slide.
caveats: The embargo's impact is compounded with the concurrent SWIFT disconnection and US NDAA oil sanctions taking effect within weeks; annual WDI series cannot cleanly separate the EU embargo's marginal contribution from these parallel measures.

caveats: The embargo's impact is compounded with the concurrent SWIFT disconnection and US NDAA oil sanctions taking effect within weeks; annual WDI series cannot cleanly separate the EU embargo's marginal contribution from these parallel measures.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Cutting major Iranian banks off SWIFT messaging severed the primary channel for settling international payments just as NDAA sanctions targeted foreign oil buyers, a combination the source material explicitly links to a sharp rial depreciation through 2012-13, which the official/parallel FX-gap and gold-coin-price series are built to capture.
caveats: This event is one leg of a near-simultaneous sanctions stack (EU embargo, SWIFT, NDAA); the 2012-13 inflation and FX moves reflect the combined shock rather than the SWIFT action in isolation.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: The rial lost roughly a quarter to 40% of its value in a single week; this is the direct, documented event the official/parallel FX-gap and gold-coin-price series exist to capture, and it fed straight through into a 30%+ CPI spike and a mechanical fall in USD-denominated GDP/GNI per capita.
caveats: USD-denominated GDP and GNI per capita fall partly as an artifact of currency conversion rather than real economic contraction; real household consumption and CPI effects are also entangled with the ongoing subsidy-reform inflation from 2010-14.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
event_title: Khamenei issues Resistance Economy general policies
justification: The Resistance Economy directive set a long-run policy goal of cutting oil-revenue dependence and promoting domestic/non-oil production, a background orientation that plausibly nudges oil-rent share of GDP and non-oil export growth over subsequent years.
caveats: This is a broad rhetorical policy framework under Article 110, not a self-executing measure with an immediate quantifiable lever; any shift in oil-dependence or export composition over the following decade is driven far more by sanctions and oil prices than by this directive itself.

chart_title: Inflation, consumer prices
justification: A 75% petrol-price hike (4,000 to 7,000 rials/litre) directly repriced a CPI-basket item and should show up as reduced growth in per-capita oil-product consumption as demand responds to the higher price; the new means-testing requirement also restructured cash-transfer administration.
caveats: Means-testing could reduce measured social-protection coverage (the opposite sign) if some households are dropped from the rolls; 2014-15 CPI is also affected by the general FX and sanctions environment, so isolating the petrol-price effect from other inflation drivers is imprecise.

lag_description: immediate anticipation effect, months ahead of implementation
justification: Signing (before sanctions relief actually took effect) shifted market expectations toward eventual sanctions relief, which can ease FX-hedging demand for gold coins and lift Tehran Stock Exchange sentiment on anticipated trade reopening.
caveats: No sanctions were actually lifted on the signing date itself (that came at Implementation Day in Jan 2016), so any market reaction in mid-2015 is purely anticipatory and easily confounded with domestic monetary conditions and oil-price moves in the same window.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: IAEA-certified compliance triggered the lifting of nuclear-related oil, banking, shipping and other sanctions and unlocked roughly $56bn in frozen assets, directly driving the well-documented 2016 rebound in oil exports, GDP growth (~12-13%), reserves, and the current account, plus renewed (if cautious) FDI and equity-market interest.
caveats: Iran's 2016 GDP surge is unusually large and partly reflects a low base after years of sanctions-driven contraction (2018-19); some of the recovery also tracks the broader 2016 oil-price stabilization rather than sanctions relief alone.

chart_title: Money Supply and Monetary Base
event_title: Unlicensed credit institutions collapse, sparking depositor protests
justification: As Caspian, Ayandeh and other unregulated credit institutions defaulted, the Central Bank absorbed depositor payouts partly through liquidity injections, a mechanism plausibly visible in accelerated monetary-base and broad-money growth over 2017-19.
caveats: Iran's money supply was already expanding rapidly through this period on fiscal deficits and general credit growth, so the credit-institution bailouts are one contributor among several rather than the dominant driver of M2/monetary-base trends.

chart_title: Expense
lag_description: same year, reconstruction spending over 1-3 years
justification: The M7.3 quake killed an estimated 630 people and destroyed or damaged over 15,500 houses, directly generating new disaster-linked internal displacement, and the government's stated recovery/reconstruction commitments would add to government expense in the following budget years.
caveats: WDI's government-expense series is a national aggregate driven overwhelmingly by non-disaster spending (subsidies, wages, debt service); a single province-level earthquake is unlikely to be visible in it except as a small, hard-to-isolate bump.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: Rouhani unifies exchange rate, bans free-market trading
lag_description: same year, policy unwound within months
justification: Pegging a single official rate at 42,000 rials/dollar and criminalizing free-market currency trading is a direct, dated intervention in exactly the series the official/parallel FX-gap chart tracks; gold-coin prices (a rial-hedge asset) also react to the ensuing scramble for hard-currency alternatives.
caveats: The peg proved unsustainable and the black-market rate continued moving against the official rate within months, so the near-term direction in the official series (narrowing) diverges from the underlying parallel-market and inflation trend (widening/rising), which is why direction is coded ambiguous.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Creating a third, market-linked exchange rate tier for exporter FX earnings formally institutionalized a multi-rate system, widening the gap tracked between the official rate and market-based rates and reshaping export-related FX incentives.
caveats: NIMA was introduced amid the broader FX crisis following the April 2018 peg and looming US withdrawal from the JCPOA, so its incremental effect apart from that broader context is hard to isolate.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Announcing US withdrawal and the reimposition of sanctions after 90/180-day wind-downs is the single most consequential event in this batch for the 2018-19 economic crisis, directly driving the rial collapse, gold-coin surge, inflation spike (among the highest in Iran's modern history), GDP contraction and export declines documented in this period; nominal TSE gains partly reflect investors seeking an inflation/FX hedge.
caveats: The withdrawal announcement, the two snapback deadlines (Aug and Nov 2018), and the 2019 oil-waiver removal form one continuous sanctions-reimposition sequence in this same event batch; attributing the full 2018-19 macro deterioration to the withdrawal announcement alone overstates a single decision within a longer process, and nominal equity gains in a hyperinflationary environment do not represent real returns.

chart_title: National Motor Vehicle Production (incl. Paykan/Iran Khodro Milestones)
justification: Executive Order 13846 specifically targeted Iran's automotive sector (alongside precious metals and rial transactions), directly threatening the foreign parts/technology partnerships (e.g., PSA, Renault) that Iranian automakers depended on, consistent with the milestone-marked production series through 2018.

event_title: Second snapback: oil and banking sanctions

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: US designates IRGC a Foreign Terrorist Organization
lag_description: gradual, chilling effect over following months
justification: Designating the IRGC (and its Khatam al-Anbia construction/engineering conglomerate) an FTO added legal risk to any residual foreign business dealings with IRGC-linked firms, marginally reinforcing the broader sanctions-driven FX pressure of 2019.
caveats: This is a diplomatic/legal designation with no direct trade or financial mechanism captured by any chart in this database; any FX effect is folded into the much larger contemporaneous oil-waiver-removal shock and cannot be separately identified.

chart_title: Fuel exports (% of merchandise exports)
event_title: US ends oil waivers -- push to zero exports
justification: Ending sanctions waivers for Iran's remaining oil buyers (China, India, Japan, South Korea, Turkey) to push exports toward zero is a direct, documented shock: exports fell from over 2.5 million bbl/day to under 1 million within months, mechanically collapsing fuel-export share, current-account receipts, oil rents and GDP growth for FY2019.
caveats: 2019 also saw the November fuel-price-hike protests and continuing NIMA/official FX distortions, so while the oil-waiver removal is the single largest documented driver of the 2019 export collapse, GDP and FX series reflect additional domestic shocks in the same year.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Placing Iran on the FATF blacklist over AML/CFT standards added compliance friction to the international banking channels that survived US sanctions, plausibly reinforcing both the FX-gap and any residual foreign-investment reluctance.
caveats: By mid-2019 Iran was already under near-total US financial and oil sanctions from the waiver removal two months earlier, so FATF's incremental marginal effect on top of that pre-existing near-total isolation is very difficult to identify separately in annual data.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
justification: The Arab oil embargo and the accompanying OPEC price actions in October 1973 roughly quadrupled world crude prices; as a major oil exporter Iran's own posted price rose in lockstep (the Shah was in fact a leading advocate for the OPEC price hikes at the Oct-Dec 1973 Tehran/Vienna meetings), mechanically lifting oil rents as a share of GDP, improving the terms of trade, and pushing the current account into surplus.
caveats: Iran's own OPEC diplomacy and unilateral price-setting were arguably a more proximate driver of the price rise than the Arab embargo itself, which Iran (non-Arab) did not join; global demand growth and US dollar weakness in this period also contributed to the price level, so the embargo is one strand among several rather than the sole cause.

chart_title: Net barter terms of trade index
justification: Saudi Arabia's abandonment of the OPEC swing-producer role in late 1985 triggered a global oil glut that roughly halved world crude prices through 1986; as an oil-dependent exporter still fighting the Iran-Iraq War, Iran's oil-rent share of GDP, goods export revenue, terms of trade and current account all took a direct hit from the same price collapse.
caveats: Iran's oil output and export capacity were also being independently degraded by wartime tanker attacks and refinery/terminal damage in this period, so the price collapse and war damage are intertwined and hard to separate in the annual data; the war itself is likely the dominant driver of the broader fiscal deterioration.

chart_title: Oil rents (% of GDP)
event_title: Iraqi invasion of Kuwait — Gulf War fiscal costs
justification: Iraq's invasion of Kuwait removed both countries' oil output from the market and briefly roughly doubled world oil prices in late 1990; Iran, though not a combatant, benefited from the same global price spike through higher oil-rent share of GDP and an improved terms of trade in annual data.
caveats: The price spike reversed within months once Saudi Arabia and other producers raised output to cover the shortfall, so the annual-average effect is modest and easily masked by Iran's own concurrent post-Iran-Iraq-War reconstruction spending and reintegration into oil markets; not a mechanism specific to Iran.

chart_title: Net domestic credit
event_title: Nationalization of banks and major industries
justification: Overnight nationalization placed the entire commercial banking system's deposits, credit and balance sheet under state control, directly restructuring the money/credit aggregates this category tracks (broad money, private-sector credit, central-bank asset share).
caveats: The 1979 revolution brought simultaneous capital flight, currency instability and administrative disruption independent of the nationalization decree itself, and several GFDD/WDI series in this category start only from 1960/61 with sparse Iran-specific data quality in the immediate post-revolution years, making it hard to isolate nationalization's own effect from the broader revolutionary shock.

event_title: Nationalization of banks and major industries
justification: Absorbing large private conglomerates into state and bonyad ownership shrank the pool of independently listed private companies and depressed Tehran Stock Exchange activity in this period.
caveats: Capital-market contraction in 1979-1981 also reflects general revolutionary uncertainty, subsequent war mobilization, and capital flight; WDI market-capitalization coverage for Iran in this era is thin, so the chart may not show a clean, attributable break.

chart_title: Total reserves minus gold
justification: Executive Order 12170 rendered roughly $12bn of Iran's foreign reserves inaccessible overnight, a direct hit to the reserves this series measures.
caveats: Total reserves reported in WDI may blend gold and other components differently than the frozen amount, and 1979 Iran data points in this series can be sparse or estimated, so the magnitude shown may not precisely match the $12bn freeze.

chart_title: Total reserves (% of total external debt)
justification: Reserves as a share of external debt would mechanically fall if a large share of gross reserves became frozen and unusable.
caveats: This ratio is also driven by debt-stock changes unrelated to the freeze, and Iran carried little external debt at this time, which limits how visibly the freeze would move this particular ratio.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: Losing access to frozen dollar reserves pushed importers and the public toward informal/parallel currency channels, widening the gap between the official and parallel exchange rate.
caveats: The freeze coincided with the hostage crisis and broader revolutionary turmoil, both of which independently pressured the parallel exchange rate, so the freeze's standalone contribution to the gap cannot be cleanly isolated.

chart_title: Merchandise exports to low- and middle-income economies in South Asia (% of total merchandise exports)
justification: A full US embargo formally cut off one of Iran's trading relationships, forcing a shift of remaining trade toward European and Asian partners.
caveats: The US was already a diminished direct trading partner after the 1979 revolution and hostage crisis, and the far larger shock of the Iran-Iraq War arrived just five months later; WDI's aggregate merchandise trade series cannot isolate the US embargo's own marginal effect from these overlapping, larger disruptions.

caveats: The US was already a diminished direct trading partner after the 1979 revolution and hostage crisis, and the far larger shock of the Iran-Iraq War arrived just five months later; WDI's aggregate merchandise trade series cannot isolate the US embargo's own marginal effect from these overlapping, larger disruptions.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
justification: A comprehensive trade embargo compounded the asset freeze five months earlier in squeezing formal dollar access, adding further pressure toward parallel-market currency trading.
caveats: This is one of several compounding 1979-1980 shocks (asset freeze, revolution, imminent war) acting on the exchange-rate gap simultaneously; the embargo's distinct marginal contribution cannot be isolated from the others.

chart_title: Military expenditure (% of GDP)
justification: An eight-year, full-scale land and air war against Iraq forced immediate and sustained mobilization of defense spending, the direct subject of this category.
caveats: Iran's official wartime defense-budget reporting is incomplete and often estimated by outside sources (e.g. SIPRI), so the magnitude in these series may understate or misstate true wartime military outlays.

chart_title: Revenue, excluding grants
lag_description: same year, worsening through the war
justification: War costs were financed heavily through central-bank borrowing and expanded budget deficits, directly shaping the government-debt and deficit series in this category (e.g. government debt to the central bank, which grows sharply over the war years).

chart_title: Sugar (Raw Equivalent)
justification: The government introduced ration coupons for sugar, rice, meat and cooking oil specifically because wartime import disruption and a foreign-exchange squeeze constrained the available supply of these staples.
caveats: FAOSTAT's national per-capita food-supply series are annual averages that also reflect population growth, income changes and harvest variation; coupon rationing itself was partly designed to smooth consumption rather than simply reduce it, so any visible drop in these series cannot be attributed to the coupon policy alone versus the underlying wartime shortage it was responding to.

chart_title: Total reserves minus gold
event_title: Algiers Accords partially unfreeze Iranian assets
justification: Unblocking roughly $7.9-8bn of the frozen $12bn in assets and transferring 50 tonnes of gold directly restored reserves.
caveats: Reserves were simultaneously being drawn down to finance the four-month-old war effort, so the net change in this series reflects both the unfreezing and concurrent wartime spending.

chart_title: U.S. Dollar Exchange Rate
event_title: Algiers Accords partially unfreeze Iranian assets
justification: Restored access to a large share of frozen dollar assets eased some of the formal FX scarcity that had been widening the parallel-market gap.
caveats: This partial relief arrived four months into full-scale war financing needs, which were independently pushing in the opposite direction, making the net effect on the FX gap ambiguous.

chart_title: Net domestic credit
justification: Restructuring the entire banking system around profit-and-loss-sharing contracts rather than fixed interest is a direct, legally mandated change to how the credit and deposit aggregates in this category are formed and priced.
caveats: Iran's banking-sector data through the mid-1980s is sparse and war-disrupted, and several series in this category may not show a clean structural break at the 1984 implementation date even though the legal framework changed.

chart_title: Real interest rate
justification: The law replaced fixed interest rates with Bank Markazi-set minimum/maximum expected profit-share rates, directly changing the mechanism this real-interest-rate series measures.
caveats: Iran's reported real interest rate under the profit-sharing system is a conceptual approximation of a non-interest instrument, and high wartime inflation independently swings the real rate regardless of the legal-framework change.

chart_title: Merchandise exports to low- and middle-income economies in South Asia (% of total merchandise exports)
event_title: US bans imports of Iranian-origin goods
justification: Barring all imports of Iranian-origin goods into the US closed off a (by then already minor) direct export channel, part of the broader tanker-war-era squeeze on Iranian trade.
caveats: By 1987 Iran's oil exports were already routed mainly through Europe and Asia and were being disrupted far more by the ongoing Tanker War and Iraqi strikes on export terminals than by this specific US import ban, so its standalone contribution to aggregate export series is likely small.

chart_title: Military expenditure (% of GDP)
event_title: Iran-Iraq War ends — ceasefire takes effect
justification: The ceasefire ended the need for sustained wartime mobilization spending, allowing military expenditure to taper from its wartime peak.
caveats: Post-war defense spending did not fall to zero given ongoing regional tensions and demobilization costs, and Iran's official military-spending reporting for this transition period remains partly estimated.

chart_title: Adjusted net national income (annual % growth)
event_title: Iran-Iraq War ends — ceasefire takes effect
justification: Ending the war removed the drag of military mobilization and infrastructure destruction, opening the way for the reconstruction-driven recovery formalized in the 1989 Five-Year Plan.
caveats: Iran's late-1980s/early-1990s GDP path was also shaped by global oil-price swings and the 1989 leadership transition after Khomeini's death, so the ceasefire's contribution to the recovery cannot be cleanly separated from these concurrent factors.

caveats: Iran's late-1980s/early-1990s GDP path was also shaped by global oil-price swings and the 1989 leadership transition after Khomeini's death, so the ceasefire's contribution to the recovery cannot be cleanly separated from these concurrent factors.

chart_title: Adjusted net national income (annual % growth)
justification: The Rafsanjani government's reconstruction-and-liberalization plan directed public investment and import liberalization toward rebuilding war-damaged capacity, supporting the early-1990s growth rebound visible in national accounts.
caveats: This recovery is difficult to separate from the simple peace dividend of the war's end fourteen months earlier and from the concurrent oil-price environment, both of which would have driven some rebound regardless of the specific Plan.

caveats: This recovery is difficult to separate from the simple peace dividend of the war's end fourteen months earlier and from the concurrent oil-price environment, both of which would have driven some rebound regardless of the specific Plan.

chart_title: Mid-Year Supplementary Budget Additions (FY1357, 1358, 1369)
justification: Post-war reconstruction spending required mid-year supplementary budget appropriations as the government ramped up rebuilding, one of the episodes this chart's FY1369 (1990/91) data point covers.
caveats: FY1369 also includes the aftermath of the June 1990 Manjil-Rudbar earthquake, whose emergency relief spending would independently require supplementary appropriations in the same fiscal year, making it hard to separate reconstruction-plan spending from disaster relief in this single chart.

chart_title: External debt stocks (% of GNI)
event_title: External debt crisis forces payment moratorium
justification: Heavy short-term postwar borrowing (external debt reaching roughly $28bn) combined with weaker oil prices to force the Central Bank into an effective payment moratorium, the direct subject of every series in this category.
caveats: Debt-stock and debt-service reporting for Iran in the early 1990s is not always consistent across sources, and the exact peak and composition of the debt varies by estimate.

caveats: Debt-stock and debt-service reporting for Iran in the early 1990s is not always consistent across sources, and the exact peak and composition of the debt varies by estimate.
